title: Untitled
content:
• Still writing up my PhD thesis and very much enjoying my Research Fellow role at the Music & Artificial Intelligence: Building Critical Interdisciplinary Studies (MusAI) research programme (see previous News item!).
  • I'll be doing a 6-month internship starting in October at Sony AI in Barcelona. I'll be working with Fabio Morreale on a project that critically studies text-to-music systems, in particular the assumption that language is a meaningful interface for representing and conditioning music generation.
  • I'll be at AI Music Studies 2026 conference (March 30th - April 1st) at University of Nottingham. I'll be delivering two paper presentations: On ghosts and slop: is there `an' AI music aesthetic (with Prof Andrew McPherson) and Beyond ethics, for a critical interdisciplinary AI music pedagogy (with Prof Georgina Born).
  • The journal paper When AI doesn't sound like AI: negotiating aesthetic expectations in technology-mediated musical practice paper I wrote in collaboration with Dr Adam Pultz Melbye and Prof Andrew McPherson has been accepted. It will be part of the Special Issue on Embedding Algorithms in Music and Sound Art at the Organised Sound Journal, forthcoming August 2026. (You can read the preprint here)
  • I'm co-chairing the NIME 2026 Workshop Track with Pete Bennett from University of Bristol. I'll be at the conference in late June in London, and I'll also be at the NIME and also I'll be at the AI Music: Critical Interdisciplinary Perspectives, Beyond Ethics pre-conference workshop we're organising with MusAI.
  • Reading/listening to The Making of the Atomic Bomb by Richard Rhodes, which was recommended to me by my academic mentor in my physics undergrad. I'm only a few chapters in, but it's so good! So far I've enjoyed learning about Polanyi's (yes, the "tacit knowledge" person – the parallels between HCI/design and STS keep showing everywhere!) ideas on the `orthodoxy' of science and its initiation rites. I'm sure someone has written about this, but it's interesting to think about the similarities between the nuclear physics of the 20th century and current AI research.

title: Untitled
content:
• Currently writing my PhD thesis. I expect to submit it in late spring and to do my PhD viva (defence) in summer. In spring I'll start looking for roles in Canada (Montreal/Toronto area) & the UK.
  • In November I started a Research Fellow part-time role at the Music & Artificial Intelligence: Building Critical Interdisciplinary Studies (MusAI) ERC project, led by Prof Georgina Born. I'm working on the "Prototyping Radically Interdisciplinary Music AI Pedagogies" work package, led by Prof Rebecca Fiebrink at the Creative Computing Institute. I'm very excited about this first post-doc role as it allows me to continue working on the STS angle to study music technology that I've very much enjoyed during my thesis.
  • In September I submitted my last PhD journal paper (fingers crossed!) on my collaboration with Adam Pultz Melbye, more info in the project page. [Update: it's been accepted!! will release a preprint soon]
  • This last autumn semester I taught Coding for Digital Media at the MSc Applied ML for Creatives (Creative Computing Institute, University of the Arts, London). I was also invited to teach a 4h class in the MRes Creative Computing on STS and HCI.
  • Because my @qmul.ac.uk email account will expire at some point in the near future, I created a personal email account at hi@teresapelinski.com (do I present techy enough if my personal email account ends in @gmail.com?). I think it was wise not to attempt to self-host my email account; I'm using Zoho, the cheapest provider (£14.40/year, 10GB hosting) I could find that didn't look too dodgy.
